top_risks: Skill command bypasses model reasoning with direct tool dispatch
Skill directly exposes a high-risk OpenClaw tool
Potential skill naming collision in scanned scope
Potential skill naming collision in scanned scope

findings.title: Skill command bypasses model reasoning with direct tool dispatch
findings.explanation: The skill config requests direct tool dispatch instead of normal model-mediated invocation.
findings.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw supports slash-command style tool dispatch that can bypass model reasoning and approval shaping for skill execution.
prerequisite_context: The finding relies on parsed SKILL.md frontmatter and normalized invocation metadata.
analyst_notes: Invocation-policy findings are specific to OpenClaw command semantics, not generic markdown content.
findings.remediation: Require careful review of command-tool targets and prefer model-mediated invocation when possible.

findings.title: Skill directly exposes a high-risk OpenClaw tool
findings.explanation: The skill directly declares or strongly implies use of the `exec` tool.
findings.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw skills can expose or guide concrete tool usage through metadata and slash-command wiring rather than only through free-form text.
prerequisite_context: Tool reachability was inferred from command-tool, requires.config, or high-confidence body guidance.
analyst_notes: Phase 4/5 tool reachability remains a structured-plus-heuristic analysis, not full data flow.
findings.remediation: Limit direct tool exposure and prefer least-privilege tool choices.

findings.title: Potential skill naming collision in scanned scope
findings.explanation: Multiple skills in the scanned scope share the same resolved name or slug candidate `slug:trusted-helper`.
findings.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw merges skills from multiple roots with source precedence, so same-name collisions can become trusted-name hijack or shadowing problems.
prerequisite_context: Collision analysis is limited to the current scan scope unless all relevant skill roots are present.
analyst_notes: Collision confidence is PossibleScopeLimited; precedence analysis avoids inventing global winner/loser claims outside the scanned roots.
findings.remediation: Rename colliding skills or explicitly review how they will resolve under OpenClaw precedence rules.

findings.title: Potential skill naming collision in scanned scope
findings.explanation: Multiple skills in the scanned scope share the same resolved name or slug candidate `trusted helper`.
findings.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw merges skills from multiple roots with source precedence, so same-name collisions can become trusted-name hijack or shadowing problems.
prerequisite_context: Collision analysis is limited to the current scan scope unless all relevant skill roots are present.
analyst_notes: Collision confidence is PossibleScopeLimited; precedence analysis avoids inventing global winner/loser claims outside the scanned roots.
findings.remediation: Rename colliding skills or explicitly review how they will resolve under OpenClaw precedence rules.

findings.title: Instruction coerces sensitive tool usage
findings.explanation: Instruction pressures use of sensitive tools: Use exec.
findings.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw skills can package behavioral instructions together with invocation, install, and tool configuration. That makes prompt-level coercion materially relevant to real tool authority, not just to text generation.
prerequisite_context: Instruction extraction produced a coercive segment.
analyst_notes: Phase 5 uses pattern- and context-based instruction analysis rather than an LLM classifier.
findings.remediation: Remove coercive language that overrides approval, safety, or trusted-instruction boundaries.

attack_paths.title: Instruction steers execution-capable tool use

steps.summary: The skill contains coercive instructions that pressure tool use.
An execution-capable tool is reachable or directly wired.
Following the instruction can produce direct system-side action.

edges.rationale: The instruction specifically points toward a sensitive tool.
The reachable tool can directly affect execution or filesystem state.

attack_paths.explanation: Prompt-level coercion combines with execution-capable tool reachability, creating a plausible execution path.
prerequisites: The instruction is followed by the operator or invoking agent.
attack_paths.impact: Execution or state-changing action through tool coercion.
inferred_nodes: The tool action leads to execution or direct state change.
attack_paths.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw skills can influence actual tool calls rather than only text output, so prompt-level instructions can become direct execution pressure.

attack_paths.title: Direct slash-command dispatch to a sensitive tool

steps.summary: The skill directly dispatches to a tool.
The tool is high-risk or state-changing.

edges.rationale: Direct dispatch bypasses normal model mediation and leads straight to tool use.

attack_paths.explanation: The skill can map operator invocation directly onto a sensitive tool surface.
prerequisites: The skill is invoked as a slash command or equivalent user-invocable action.
attack_paths.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw supports slash-command style direct tool dispatch for skills, making invocation metadata part of the security boundary.

attack_paths.title: Naming collision can become trust hijack

steps.summary: The scanned scope contains a same-name or same-slug collision.
The collided skill also carries risky install or invocation behavior.

edges.rationale: A naming collision increases the chance that risky behavior is mistaken for a trusted skill.

attack_paths.explanation: Naming collisions can turn risky behavior into a trusted-name hijack when operators assume they are invoking the expected skill.
prerequisites: OpenClaw precedence resolves the colliding skill in favor of the risky version.
attack_paths.impact: Trusted-name confusion leading to risky install or invocation.
inferred_nodes: Actual resolution depends on broader OpenClaw source scope beyond the current scan.
attack_paths.why_openclaw_specific: OpenClaw merges multiple skill roots with precedence, so naming collisions are operationally meaningful, not cosmetic.
